coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot modified polyacrylonitrile fibers: Recyclable materials capable of selectively and reversibly adsorbing small-sized anionic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bin
Chen, Pei-Yao
Zhao, Rong-Xu
Zhang, Lei
Chen, Yu
Yu, Li-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Amine-rich carbon dot (CD) functionalized polyacrylonitrile fibers (PANF-g-CDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. These fibers are fluorescent and contain nano-sized pores. Dye adsorption experiments demonstrated that PANF-g-CDs preferred to adsorb small-sized anionic dyes with high aspect ratio, such as methyl orange (MO). The maximum adsorption capacity for MO reached 422 mg/g. The adsorption kinetics satisfied the pseudo second-order adsorption model, and the adsorption behavior was monolayer uniform adsorption. After adsorption, PANF-g-CDs could be separated conveniently just by taking it out from the solution. Moreover, the used PANF-g-CDs could be regenerated in an alkaline aqueous solution very rapidly within 30 min. Its adsorption performance was so stable that it did not show obvious performance loss even after ten adsorption-desorption cycles. In addition, the fluorescence of the fiber exhibited periodical change during the adsorption-desorption processes. Thus, this CD modified fibers with the combined advantages of simple synthesis, high dye-adsorption efficiency and selectivity, moderate dye-adsorption rate, fast dye-desorption, easy recyclability, stable adsorption performance and photoluminescent nature could be promising adsorbents for practical dyestuff wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyacrylonitrile fibers
Dye adsorption
Recyclable adsorbent
Selective adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dye desorption and reuse of PANF-g-CD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra and (B) XRD spectra of PANF and PANF-g-CDs with different weight gains.
SEM images of PANF and PANF-g-CDs.

Typical photos of PANF-g-CD under the irradiation of (A) visible and (B) UV light; (C) Influence of λex on the maximum emission intensity and λem, max of PANF-g-CD, and (D) Influence of weight gain on the maximum emission intensity and λem, max of PANF-g-CD (PANF-g-CD0.62 was used as the representative).
Influence of pH on the adsorption of the fiber (the concentration of MO is 0.2 mM, and the volume is 20 mL, the mount of PANF-g-CD0.30 is 20 mg).
Influence of weight gain of the fiber on the adsorption capacity of MO (20 mg fiber was pre-treated at pH 5, the initial concentration of MO is 5 mM, and the volume is 20 mL).

(A) Adsorption ratio of PANF-g-CD for different dyes, (B) photos of the dye solution before adsorption, and (C) photos of the dye solution after adsorption (20 mg of PANF-g-CD0.30 that was pretreated with pH 5 was used).
Time-dependent UV–Vis spectra of mixture solution of (A) MO/MB and (B) MO/DB in the presence of PANF-g-CD (20 mg of PANF-g-CD0.30 that was pretreated with pH 5 was used).
Influence of pH on the desorption of MO from PANF-g-CD0.30.
Influence of the adsorption-desorption cycle on (A) the adsorption efficiency of PANF-g-CD0.30 for MO and (B) the enriching ratio of the fiber.
Photos of PANF-g-CDs during the adsorption-desorption process.
Influence of weight gain on the mechanical properties, surface area and average pore radius of PANF-g-CD.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot modified polyacrylonitrile fibers: Recyclable materials capable of selectively and reversibly adsorbing small-sized anionic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling, School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling, School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Surface-Earth System Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Surface-Earth System Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amine-rich carbon dot (CD) functionalized polyacrylonitrile fibers (PANF-g-CDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. These fibers are fluorescent and contain nano-sized pores. Dye adsorption experiments demonstrated that PANF-g-CDs preferred to adsorb small-sized anionic dyes with high aspect ratio, such as methyl orange (MO). The maximum adsorption capacity for MO reached 422 mg/g. The adsorption kinetics satisfied the pseudo second-order adsorption model, and the adsorption behavior was monolayer uniform adsorption. After adsorption, PANF-g-CDs could be separated conveniently just by taking it out from the solution. Moreover, the used PANF-g-CDs could be regenerated in an alkaline aqueous solution very rapidly within 30 min. Its adsorption performance was so stable that it did not show obvious performance loss even after ten adsorption-desorption cycles. In addition, the fluorescence of the fiber exhibited periodical change during the adsorption-desorption processes. Thus, this CD modified fibers with the combined advantages of simple synthesis, high dye-adsorption efficiency and selectivity, moderate dye-adsorption rate, fast dye-desorption, easy recyclability, stable adsorption performance and photoluminescent nature could be promising adsorbents for practical dyestuff wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes, as a kind of prevalent pollutant, are widely used in textiles, paper, plastics, pharmaceuticals and food industries [1]. Its annual output has exceeded 7 × 105 tons, and more than 10 percent of these dyes are considered to be discharged into the environment during dyeing processes [2], which causes serious environment problems, especially water pollution [3]. Numerous techniques have been developed to treat the dye-containing effluents, such as solvent extraction [4], membrane separation [5–7], adsorption [8–10], biodegradation [11] and photocatalytic degradation [12–15]. Among these methods, adsorption is generally recognized as the most feasible one due to its simple fabrication, high efficiency and cost-effectiveness [16,17].

Varieties of adsorbents have been constructed for dyestuff wastewater treatment, such as polymeric gels [18], minerals [19,20], agricultural wastes [21–23], metal organic gels [16,24] and carbon materials including activated carbon [25–27], carbon nanotubes [28–30] and graphene [17,31–33]. Among these materials, carbon-based materials have become an important class of adsorbents due to their specific features [34]. Their large surface areas endow them high adsorption capacities, and their high thermal, physical and chemical stabilities ensure their long-lasting performance in real water treatment. Jafa prepared a series of metal organic framework hydride nanocomposites based on nanotube and graphene [35]. The specific surface area of these materials reached 1485 m2/g, and the maximum adsorption capacity was superior high with a value of 3300 mg/g for malachite green.

Beside the request for high adsorption capacity, adsorption materials which can be recycled after usage is also in demand [36–40]. Liu prepared a Fe3O4 nanoparticles-functionalized activated carbon, which showed high adsorption capacity and rapid magnetic separation, and it could be regenerated by washing with alcohol [25]. Cui constructed a class of graphene oxide/hydrophobically associated polyacrylamide composite hydrogels, and this recyclable adsorbents showed superior mechanical strength and high toughness [32]. In addition, functionalized fibers can be also used as recyclable adsorbents, and they show many advantages, such as good mechanical stability, large specific surface area and easiness to be woven into different shapes. In our previous work, we prepared a series of zwitterionic polyacrylonitrile fibers (PANF). These fibers could adsorb different type of dyes and released their cargos in a small amount of water to achieve the regeneration [41,42].

Carbon dot (CD), as a zero-dimensional carbon material, has attracted massive research interest in the past decade [43]. It shows many unique properties, including fascinating photoluminescent performance, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost and simple synthetic routes [44]. These features make it wide application in optoelectronics devices [45,46], biological labeling [47], and sensors [48]. Beyond the aforementioned application fields, recently CDs have been developed as adsorbents for the removal of organic pollutants [49–55]. Several research groups have ever prepared different kinds of CD-modified magnetic nanocomposites to remove organic pollutants, such as benzo[a]pyrene with an adsorption capacity of 76.23 ng/mg [49], carbamazepine with an adsorption capacity of 65 mg/g [50], methyl orange with an adsorption capacity of 181.2 mg/g [51], tetracycline with an adsorption capacity of 591.72 mg/g [52], methyl blue with an adsorption capacity of ca. 18 mg/g [53]. Furthermore, PET/MWNT/CD nanocomposite and CD modified mesoporous organosilica have been also used for the removal of methylene blue with an adsorption capacity of 1.66 mg/g [54] and 2,4-dichlorophenol with an adsorption capacity of 99.70 mg/g [55], respectively. Comparing these achievements on the removal of organic pollutants by the CD-modified materials, it could be found that the materials having a relatively high adsorption capacity normally required long time to reach the adsorption equilibrium. For instance, it took more than 10 h for NiFe2O4-CDs to adsorb more than 90% of tetracycline from water [52]. Only 80% of carbamazepine was removed by CD-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes during the initial 3 h [50]. As for CD modified mesoporous organosilica, more than 3 h was also required to remove 90% of 2,4-dichlorophenol. For the practical application, besides high adsorption capacity, fast adsorption and fast regeneration are highly desired, whereas no description on regeneration rate could be found in these literatures. Moreover, the photoluminescence property of CDs was totally ignored. In this contribution, a new class of recyclable dye adsorbents based on CD and PANF were constructed, which showed many advantages, including high adsorption capacity, selectively adsorption ability, moderate adsorption rate, easy and fast regeneration, excellent recyclability for at least ten-times reuse, photoluminescent nature to monitor the dye adsorption-desorption process. To our best knowledge, adsorbents simultaneously possessing so many advantages are few.

Hyperbranched polyethylenimine (HPEI, M n = 1.0 × 104 g/mol) was purchased from Aldrich. Citric acid (CA), hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide and potassium carbonate were obtained from Tianjin Kewei Chemical Company. PANF was purchased from Fushun Petrochemical Corporation. Ten different dyes including Methyl Orange (MO), Sodium Fluorescein (FS), Sunset Yellow (SY), Cresol Red (CrR), Alizarin Red (AR), Eosin Y (EY), Direct Blue (DB), Congo Red (CR), Crystal Violet (CV), and Methylene Blue (MB) were purchased from Guangfu Chemical Company and used directly.

Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer was used to record the 1H NMR spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with an ESCALBA Xi+ photoelectron spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation at 250 W. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was recorded by a JEOL JEM-2100F instrument. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on a Nicolet 5 DXC FT-IR spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded on a BDX 3300 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The surface of the fibers was characterized by a model XL-30 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Elemental analysis was performed on Elementar Vario EL III. The mechanical properties of the fibers were studied using a testing machine (EZ TEST, Shimadzu), and the fibers were woven to give a sample with dimensions of 1.5 mm × 1.5 mm × 100 mm. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were carried out at 77 K on a NOVA2200e pore size and surface area analyzer. Varian Gary Eclipse photoluminescence spectrometer was used to record the fluorescence spectra of the fiber. The UV–Vis spectra of the dye solutions were recorded by a Purkinje General T6 UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

CD was synthesized according to a reported method with a slight modification [56]. 1.50 g of HPEI and 0.50 g of CA were mixed with 12 mL of de-ionized water to form a transparent solution, and then the solution was transferred to a 25-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h to obtain the desired CD. After cooling to room temperature, 0.40 g of PANF was put into the autoclave, and another 4 mL of de-ionized water was added to ensure that the fiber was immersed in the liquid. Subsequently, the autoclave was sealed up and heated in the oven at 140 °C for a certain time. After cooling down, the fiber was filtered out and washed with hot water (~60 °C) several times till neutral. The obtained fiber was dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight. The grafting efficiency was expressed as the weight gain of the fiber, and calculated according to the following equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
W e i g h t
G a i n =
W -
W
0
W
0
× 100 %
where W 0 and W 1 are the weight of the raw fiber and modified fiber,respectively. Fig. S1 shows the influence of reaction time on the weight gain.

For the subsequent dye adsorption experiments, the obtained PANF-g-CDs might be required for the treatment with different pH of solution. The detail was as follows: Certain amount of PANF-g-CD was soaked in de-ionized water, and then the pH of the solution was adjusted to a certain value by HCl or NaOH solution. After being stirred for about 1 h, the fiber was filtered out and dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

Dye adsorption: After being adjusted to the desired pH, certain amount of PANF-g-CD was put into 20 mL of aqueous dye solution, and stirred gently at 25 °C. The absorbance and the mole extinction coefficient of the dye solution was acquired by a UV–Vis spectrometer, and then these data were used to monitor the dye concentration in water at different time. Dye absorption amount (q t) was calculated according to the following equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
q t
=
(
C 0
-
C t
) × V × M
m
where q t is the amount of dye adsorbed by the fiber at time t (mg/g), C 0 and C t are the dye concentration before and after adsorption (mmol/L). V is the volume of the dye solution (L), M is the molecular weight of the dye (g/mol), m is the weight of the fiber (g).

Selective adsorption for the mixed dye solutions: For the determination of adsorption capacities of PANF-g-CD for different dyes, PANF-g-CD (20 mg) that was pretreated with pH 5 aqueous solution was soaked into 20 mL of dye aqueous solution (C 0 = 65 μg/mL). After being gently stirred for 2 h, the concentration of the solution was acquired (C 1). The adsorption ratio was calculated according to the following equation (Eq. (3)):
(3)
A d s o r p t i o n
R a t i o =
C
0
-
C
1
C
0
× 100 %

For the adsorption of MO from mixed dyes solutions, PANF-g-CD (20 mg) that was pretreated with pH 5 aqueous solution was soaked into 20 mL of MO/MB mixture solution (MO = 0.1 mM, MB = 0.1 mM). The mixture was stirred at 25 °C, the absorbance of the dye solution was monitored every five minutes by a UV–Vis spectrometer. The selective adsorption experiment for MO/DB was carried out under the same condition.

Dye desorption: PANF-g-CD (20 mg, pH 5) was soaked into MO aqueous solution (20 mL, 0.2 mM), and stirred for 2 h at 25 °C, and then the fiber was taken out and put into another beaker that contained 20 mL of buffer solution with certain pHs. The mixture was gently stirred. UV–Vis spectrometer was used to monitor the adsorption and desorption process.

Reuse of the fiber: PANF-g-CD (20 mg, pH 5) was put into MO aqueous solution (100 mL, 0.02 mM). After being stirred for 2 h, the fiber was taken out, and the MO residual quantity in the solution was recorded by a UV–Vis spectrometer. The fiber was put into 10 mL of fresh KHCO3/K2CO3 buffer solution (pH 11.5, 0.02 M). After stirring for another 0.5 h, the MO concentration of the solution was recorded, and the fiber was filtered out and dried with filter paper. This adsorption-desorption cycle was carried out 10 times.

CD functionalized with plenty of amine groups was successfully prepared from the mixture of CA and HPEI under the hydrothermal condition [56,57], which was verified by 1H NMR, XPS and TEM (Figs. S2–S4). For preparing PANF-g-CDs, hydrothermally treating the mixture of PANF and CD aqueous solution at 140 °C was carried out for certain time. Under this condition, portion of the cyano groups on PANF would be converted to amide groups due to the amidation and hydrolysis reactions among cyano groups of PANF, amine groups of CD and water molecules [41], resulting in the grafting of CDs onto the fibers (Scheme 1 ).

Fig. 1 A shows the FT-IR spectra of the obtained PANF-g-CDs. Compared with the raw PANF (Fig. 1A, curve 1), many new absorption signals appear in the spectra of PANF-g-CDs. For instance, the strong absorption centered at 1661 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO stretching bond of amide groups, which indicates that CDs have been grafted onto PANF successfully; new peaks at 1391 cm−1 and 1563 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching bonds of COO−; new peak centered at around 3300 cm−1 can ascribed to N–H asymmetric stretching bong of amide groups and primary and secondary amine groups. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1B) of PANF shows a strong diffraction peak at 17.1°, which represents the X-ray reflections of the (1 0 0) crystallographic plane [58]. After modification by CDs, the intensity of this peak decreases significantly. As more CDs are grafted, the peak becomes weaker. This indicates that part of the inner crystal region of PANF is broken during the modification process.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of PANF and the modified fibers. It can be seen that the PANFs swell up after modification. The average diameter of the fiber becomes larger when more CDs are grated. When comparing the enlarged images of these fibers, the surface of the fiber with weight gain less than 30% are similar to that of the original PANF, whereas the surface of PANF-g-CD0.55 is much more rough, thus it can be concluded that high weight gain impairs the fiber to some extent.

Elemental analysis was carried out for PANF and the as-prepared PANF-g-CDs (Table 1 ). All the fibers contain the four elements of C, H, N and O. As for PANF, its O element comes from the small amount of methyl acrylate co-monomeric units. With the grafting of CDs onto PANF, the contents of H and O elements increase obviously, whereas the contents of C and N elements drop a little. As for the PANF-g-CDs with different weight gain, only the content of O element shows marked increase with the increase of weight gain. The cyano groups of PANF may be transformed to amide or carboxylic acid groups during the hydrothermal treatment. The formation of amide groups does not reduce the content of N element in the fiber, but increases the content of O element. Whereas the formation of carboxylic acid groups lowers the content of N element in the fiber and increases the content of O element markedly. Therefore, the obvious content increase of O element upon increasing the weight gain of fibers is ascribed to the transformation of certain amount of cyano groups into carboxylic acid groups that can be proved by the FT-IR analysis. If cyano groups were only transformed into carboxylic acid groups, not amide groups, the content of N element in fiber would decrease markedly. However, this is not the case according to the data in Table 1. Therefore, it can be deduced that under the hydrothermal treatment certain amount of cyano groups of fibers are transformed into amide and carboxylic acid groups separately. The amide groups bridge PANF and CDs together. It should be noted that under the preparation condition adopted here, the hydrothermal treating PANF without CD participation only resulted in slight weight gain increase, which excluded the possibility that CDs were not grafted onto the fiber.

The mechanical properties of PANF-g-CDs were measured, and the results were listed in Table 2 . Grafting CDs onto PANF results in the decrease of maximal stress of the fiber. However, when the weight gain is not too high, the mechanical strength of the fiber is still considerable. For example, the maximal stress of PANF-g-CD with 30% weight gain is 220.1 MPa that is 76% of the raw fiber. As for the maximal strain, it increases when more CDs are grafted onto the fiber. The porosity and surface area of PANF-g-CDs were measured from nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms. It is found that these fibers contain nanometer size pores, and their average pore radius are similar to the raw PANF. Furthermore, the grafting of CDs onto PANF will reduce the surface area of the fiber slightly.

CD has been addressed to show strong fluorescence under suitable conditions [56], thus whether the obtained PANF-g-CDs in this work were also photoluminescent were checked. PANF-g-CDs are yellowish under visible light (Fig. 3 A), and can emit blue light under UV irradiation (Fig. 3B). The influence of excitation wavelength (λex) on the maximum emission intensity and maximum emission wavelength (λem, max) is shown in Fig. 3C and Fig. S5A. When λex changes from 300 to 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity increases pronouncedly, while λem, max remains unchanged. Further increasing λex will result in a red-shift and depression of the emission intensity. This behavior is similar to its precursor CD, and can be ascribed to the surface state affecting the band gap of CDs [56]. The influence of weight gain on the maximum emission intensity and λem, max of PANF-g-CDs is shown in Fig. 3D and Fig. S5B. It is clear that the fluorescence of PANF is negligible. With the weight gain increase, the fluorescence intensity of the modified fiber increases at first, but is followed with a depression. The emission spectra show slight red-shift, which may be ascribed to the extension of electronic conjugation when more CDs are grafted onto the fiber.

After being modified by amine-rich CDs, there exist lots of amino groups on the surface of the fiber, therefore, PANF-g-CDs might adsorb anionic dyes from water. Herein, MO was selected as a typical anionic dye to probe the adsorption performance of such fibers, and a UV–Vis spectrometer was used to monitor the adsorption process. Without any pre-treatment, PANF-g-CDs could adsorb MO from water, but it took several hours to reach the adsorption equilibrium (Fig. S6). Subsequently, the pH effect on the adsorption performance of PANF-g-CDs was explored (Fig. 4 and Fig. S7). When the fiber is pre-treated by alkaline aqueous solution, its adsorption ability is quite poor. However, pre-treating the fiber with acidic aqueous solution leads to a better adsorption performance. For instance, pre-treating the fiber with pH 5 solution can both increase the equilibrium adsorption amount (~3.1 times) and shorten the equilibrium adsorption time (~8.5 times) when being compared with the non-pretreated one.

To elucidate the adsorption kinetic process, the plots of Fig. 4 were fitted by pseudo first-order adsorption model and pseudo second-order adsorption model respectively. For the pseudo first-order adsorption model, it can be expressed as Eq. (4): [59]
(4)
ln
q t
=
k 1
t + l n
q e
where t is the adsorption time; q t is the adsorption amount at time t; q e is the equilibrium adsorption amount and k 1 is the adsorption constant rate.

As for the pseudo second-order adsorption model, it is expressed as Eq. (5):[41]
(5)
1
q t
=
1
ht
+
1
q e
where h (h = k2q e 2) is the initial adsorption rate constant; k2 is the overall rate constant. The plots in Fig. 4 are changed into plots of lnq t versus t and 1/q t versus 1/t for these two adsorption models, and the fitting values are provided in Table S1. The q e values calculated according the pseudo second-order adsorption model are very close to the measured ones, and a linear relationship with high correlation coefficients between 1/q t and 1/t indicates the applicability of the pseudo seconder-order model to describe the adsorption process. The fiber’s adsorption performance under acidic condition is explained as follows: MO can interact with the fiber through electrostatic attraction between the sulfonic group and the protonated amine groups. Moreover, the π-π interaction between the aromatic ring in MO and the conjugated structure existing in the CD moieties further promotes the adsorption process.

The adsorption isotherm curve of PANF-g-CD for MO was also measured (Fig. S8). At the beginning, q e increases with the increase of MO concentration, and then reaches a plateau. The equilibrium adsorption behavior is analyzed by three isotherm models, including Langmuir, Freundlich and Dubinin-Radushkevich (Fig. S9–S11) [35]. It is found that the Langmuir isotherm model fits the experimental data best (R2 = 0.9964). This indicates that the adsorption of MO by PANF-g-CD is monolayer uniform adsorption.

The adsorption capacities of PANF-g-CDs with different weight gains were measured and shown in Fig. 5 . It is clear that the adsorption capacity of PANF (weight gain = 0) is quite poor. After modification, HPEI-g-CDs can adsorb MO from water, and their adsorption capacities increase with raising the weight gains. Such phenomenon can be explained as follows: the CD moieties on PANF-g-CDs are the effective adsorption sites for MO, therefore, that more CDs are grafted onto PANF favors to adsorb more MO molecules. The maximum adsorption capacity for MO is measured to be 422 mg/g. Compared with other reported adsorbents, this value is close to the up-to-date maximum value for MO (Table S2). The adsorption capacity of PANF-g-CD was further compared with HPEI modified PANF (PANF-g-HPEI) [41]. It is found that the q e of PANF-g-CD is about two-fold higher than that of PANF-g-HPEI with a similar weight gain. This result indicates that for the adsorption process of PANF-g-CDs, except the ionic interaction, the π-π interaction between the aromatic ring in MO and the conjugated structure existed in the CD moieties is crucial.

According to the above experiments, PANF-g-CDs have been proved to be an excellent adsorbent for MO. In this section, the adsorption capacities of PANF-g-CDs for some other dyes, including seven anionic dyes: FS, SY, CrR, AR, EY, DB, CR, and two cationic dyes: CV and MB were explored. The molecular structure information of these dyes are shown in Tables S3 and S4, and the adsorption results are shown in Fig. 6 . Obviously, PANF-g-CD almost can’t adsorb cationic dyes CV and MB. It is reasonable that electrostatic repulsion between the protonated amine groups on the fiber and the cationic group on the dye molecules restricts the adsorption process. For the other anionic dyes, the adsorption capacities of the fiber are also different. PANF-g-CD can adsorb MO, FS, SY and AR efficiently, and the adsorption ratios are more than 80%. But for CrR and EY, the adsorption ratios are around 40%, and as for CR and DR, the adsorption ratios are less than 10%.

In order to reveal the different adsorption behavior of PANF-g-CD for anionic dyes, we set our point on the molecule sizes of these dyes. Density functional theory was performed to calculate the molecule size using Gaussian 09 software. The geometries of these dyes were optimized at the B3LYP/6–31 + G* level of theory in the gas phase, and the results were shown in Tables S3 and S4. The molecule size of DB and CR are much bigger than other dyes. MO, FS, SY and AR are smaller. Their molecule lengths are 1.82 to 1.34 nm, and their molecule widths are 1.18 to 0.88 nm. EY and CrR have similar molecule volumes to the previous four dyes, but their aspect ratios are smaller. Their molecule lengths and widths are in the range of 1.58 to 1.35 nm. The pore size and surface area analysis (Table 2) have proved that PANF-g-CD0.30 contains nano-sized pores, and its average pore radius is ~1.62 nm. This value is comparable to the molecule sizes of the dyes. According to these results, the adsorption performance of PANF-g-CD for different anionic dyes is deduced as follows: the fiber contains nano-sized pores, and the adsorption sites are distributed in the surface and inner part of the fiber. For the dyes with big molecule volumes (DB and CR), only the surface adsorption sites on the fiber are favorable to adsorb them, which results in the poor adsorption capacity for such dyes. As for CrR and EY, their molecule lengths and widths are similar to the pore radius. Although they can permeate into the fiber, the pores are easy to be blocked by them. So the adsorption ability of PANF-g-CD for these dyes are yet not very good. For MO, FS, SY and AR, their molecule volumes are smaller, and their molecule widths are much smaller than the pore radius of the fiber, so these dyes are easy to permeate deeper inside the fiber. As a result, PANF-g-CD can adsorb these dyes efficiently. In conclusion, PANF-g-CD prepared in this work can adsorb small-sized anionic dyes with high aspect ratio efficiently.

As PANF-g-CD pre-treated by pH 5 solution showed different adsorption ability for different dyes, we explored whether this fiber could realize the selective adsorption of certain dye from dye mixtures (Fig. S12). Firstly, the mixture of cationic MB and anionic MO was used as the target of selective adsorption. Fig. 7 A shows UV–Vis spectra of MO/MB mixture solution during the adsorption process. With the time elapse, the absorbance of MO gradually decreases, while the absorbance of MB remains unchanged. This phenomenon indicates that PANF-g-CD disrupts the MO/MB complexes through adsorbing the anionic MO molecules and the decomplexed cationic MB molecules are left in the water. MO and MB have the similar size, but they carry opposite charge (see Tables S3 and S4). PANF-g-CD pre-treated by pH 5 solution possesses many protonated amine groups that can have strong electrostatic attraction force with the negative-charged MO molecules, but strong electrostatic repulsion force with the positive-charged MB molecules. This leads to the selective MO adsorption from the MO/MB mixture once PANF-g-CD is used as adsorbent. Subsequently, the mixture of MO and DB was attempted as the target of selective adsorption. A similar result is observed that MO is selectively adsorbed from MO/DB mixture (Fig. 7B). Different from the mixture of MO/MB, all the molecules in the MO/DB mixture possess the negative charge. DB carries four negative charges, whereas MO only carries one. If electrostatic attraction force were the dominating factor for the adsorption by PANF-g-CD, DB molecules would be adsorbed preferentially. However, opposite phenomenon is observed, indicating that electrostatic attraction force is not the reason for the selective MO adsorption. From the aforementioned results on the sizes of MO and DB (see Tables S3 and S4), it is clear that MO has a much smaller size than the pores of PANF-g-CD, whereas DB has a bigger size than the pores of PANF-g-CD. Therefore, MO molecules are able to permeate deeper inside the fiber, whereas DB cannot, resulting in the selective MO adsorption from the MO/DB mixture. These selective adsorption experiments further verified that such PANF-g-CDs can selective adsorb anionic dyes with small molecule volume from dyes mixture solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye desorption and reuse of PANF-g-CD
ce:section.ce:para: From the above discussion, it is known that the adsorption ability of the fiber that was pre-treated by alkaline solution was quite poor (Fig. 4). Inspired by this phenomenon, we tried to achieve the desorption of dyes in alkaline aqueous solution. PANF-g-CD0.30 with adsorbed MO was put into the buffer solutions with different pHs, and gently stirred. The desorption kinetic curves are shown in Fig. 8 . It is found that a more basic condition is in favor of the release of dyes. When the pH of the desorption solution is 11.5, more than 95% of MO on the fiber can be rapidly released within 30 min. At pH 11.5, the protonated amine groups on PANF-g-CD will be converted to the neutral amine groups completely, and the electrostatic attraction between the fiber and MO will disappear. What’s more, the carboxylic acid groups on the fiber will be converted to carboxylate anion groups, and the electrostatic repulsion between these carboxylate anion groups and the sulfonic group on MO will promote the desorption of MO from PANF-g-CD.

As a wastewater adsorbent, its recyclability is also crucial. In this section, PANF-g-CD0.30 was used to adsorb MO in low concentration, and then desorb MO in a small amount of water to achieve the enriching of dyes. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the adsorption performance of the fiber does not show much change during the recycling processes. After ten cycles, the adsorption efficiency is still over 95%. As for the enriching experiment, the volume ratio of dye solution to desorption solution was set to 10:1, so the ideal enriching ratio is 10. According to Fig. 9B, it is found that except the first cycle, the enriching ratios are always higher than 9. Moreover, the mechanical and chemical stability is also important for recyclable adsorbents. The weight loss of PANF-g-CD after ten cycles is measured to be 8.5%, which indicates that PANF-g-CD prepared in this work is quite stable during the adsorption-desorption process. We compared the recyclability of PANF-g-CD with other adsorbents reported in literatures (listed in Table S2), and found that PANF-g-CD showed many advantages: (1) Water was used as the desorption medium in this work, which was more environmentally benign than the most commonly used organic solvent; (2) The regeneration of PANF-g-CD could be achieved rapidly within 30 min, thus the recovery of adsorbent was more efficient; (3) The adsorption performance of PANF-g-CD was quite stable during the adsorption-desorption cycles.

The fluorescence changes of PANF-g-CD were also observed during the adsorption-desorption processes (Fig. 10 ). The fiber showed bright fluorescence under a UV lamp at the beginning. However, after adsorption, the yellowish fiber turned orange-red, and no fluorescence could be detected. The fluorescence quenching here can be attributed to the inner-filter effect of MO. After the desorption process, the fiber turns yellowish, and its fluorescence is recovered. What’s more, the fluorescence of PANF-g-CD goes periodical off-on change during the adsorption-desorption process. After five cycles, the fluorescence intensity is still 82% of the initial fiber. Therefore, this fluorescence change of PANF-g-CD can be used as a reliable route to monitor the dye adsorption-desorption processes.

In this work, PANF-g-CD was constructed using a hydrothermal method. This fiber showed good mechanical and chemical stability, and inherited the fluorescence properties from its precursor. It could adsorb small-sized anionic dyes with high aspect ratio efficiently. The adsorption kinetics satisfied the pseudo second-order adsorption model, and the adsorption behavior was monolayer uniform adsorption. The maximum adsorption capacity for MO was considerable and reached 422 mg/g. After adsorption, PANF-g-CD could be easily regenerated, and reused for ten times without pronounced loss of adsorption ability. In addition, the periodical off-on fluorescence change of the fiber during the adsorption-desorption processes could be used as a reliable route to monitor the dye treating process.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21875159, 21976130) and Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (18JCYBJC86800, 18JCYBJC23400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123484.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
